title: The Sound of Closing Shops at Night
text_zh: 每天晚上十点左右，我住的那条老街会进入一种独特的声音仪式。最先关门的总是卖文具的林阿姨。她拉下铁卷帘的时候，金属碰撞声在空荡的巷子里回响，像是一天疲惫的叹息。紧接着是隔壁的水果摊，老板把没卖完的西瓜搬进店里，塑料箱子拖过水泥地面发出低沉的摩擦声。然后是对面的裁缝铺，缝纫机终于停了，那种持续了一整天的嗡嗡声突然消失，反而让人觉得耳朵里空了一块。最后关门的永远是巷口的馄饨店。老板娘把最后一锅汤倒掉的声音，混合着水龙头冲洗锅具的哗哗声，构成了这条街的晚安曲。我曾经觉得这些声音是噪音，是阻碍入睡的干扰。但搬到新城区的高层公寓以后，夜晚安静得几乎令人窒息。双层隔音玻璃把一切都挡在了外面，连风声都听不到。我才意识到，那些关店的声音从来不是噪音——它们是一种陪伴，是这个街区在告诉你：今天结束了，大家都平安地收了工，你可以安心睡了。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān wǎnshang shí diǎn zuǒyòu, wǒ zhù de nà tiáo lǎo jiē huì jìnrù yī zhǒng dútè de shēngyīn yíshì. Zuì xiān guān mén de zǒng shì mài wénjù de Lín āyí. Tā lā xià tiě juǎnlián de shíhou, jīnshǔ pèngzhuàng shēng zài kōngdàng de xiàngzi lǐ huíxiǎng, xiàng shì yī tiān píbèi de tànxī. Jǐn jiēzhe shì gébì de shuǐguǒ tān, lǎobǎn bǎ méi mài wán de xīguā bān jìn diàn lǐ, sùliào xiāngzi tuō guò shuǐní dìmiàn fāchū dīchén de mócā shēng. Ránhòu shì duìmiàn de cáiféng pù, féngrènjī zhōngyú tíng le, nà zhǒng chíxù le yī zhěng tiān de wēngwēng shēng tūrán xiāoshī, fǎn'ér ràng rén juéde ěrduo lǐ kōng le yī kuài. Zuìhòu guān mén de yǒngyuǎn shì xiàngkǒu de húntun diàn. Lǎobǎnniáng bǎ zuìhòu yī guō tāng dào diào de shēngyīn, hùnhé zhe shuǐlóngtóu chōngxǐ guōjù de huāhuā shēng, gòuchéng le zhè tiáo jiē de wǎn'ān qǔ. Wǒ céngjīng juéde zhèxiē shēngyīn shì zàoyīn, shì zǔ'ài rùshuì de gānrǎo. Dàn bān dào xīn chéngqū de gāocéng gōngyù yǐhòu, yèwǎn ānjìng de jīhū lìng rén zhìxī. Shuāngcéng géyīn bōli bǎ yīqiè dōu dǎng zài le wàimiàn, lián fēngshēng dōu tīng bú dào. Wǒ cái yìshí dào, nàxiē guān diàn de shēngyīn cónglái bú shì zàoyīn——tāmen shì yī zhǒng péibàn, shì zhège jiēqū zài gàosù nǐ: jīntiān jiéshù le, dàjiā dōu píng'ān de shōu le gōng, nǐ kěyǐ ānxīn shuì le.
text_en: Every night around ten o'clock, the old street where I lived would enter a distinctive sonic ritual. The first to close was always Auntie Lin's stationery shop. When she pulled down the metal rolling shutter, the clash of metal echoed through the empty alley like the weary sigh of a long day. Right after came the fruit stand next door — the owner carrying unsold watermelons inside, plastic crates dragging across the concrete floor with a low grinding sound. Then the tailor's shop across the way, the sewing machine finally silenced, that all-day hum vanishing so suddenly it felt like a hollow space opened in your ears. The last to close was always the wonton shop at the mouth of the alley. The owner pouring out the final pot of broth, mingled with the rush of tap water rinsing the cookware — together they composed the street's goodnight song. I once thought these sounds were noise, disturbances that kept me from falling asleep. But after moving to a high-rise apartment in the new district, the nights were so quiet they felt almost suffocating. Double-paned soundproof glass blocked everything out; I couldn't even hear the wind. Only then did I realize those closing-shop sounds were never noise — they were companionship, the neighborhood telling you: the day is over, everyone has safely finished work, you can rest easy now.

title: Morning Fog over a River
text_zh: 小时候住在江边的外婆家，每天清晨我都会被一种奇特的安静叫醒——不是闹钟，不是鸡鸣，而是雾气降临时世界突然变得格外沉寂的那种感觉。推开窗户，江面上铺着一层乳白色的薄雾，对岸的房屋和树木只剩下模糊的轮廓，像一幅还没画完的水墨画。外婆说，雾是江水在呼吸。夜里水温比空气高，江面蒸腾出的水汽在冷空气中凝结，就形成了雾。她用最朴素的语言，无意中给了我一个相当准确的气象学解释。雾中的声音也会发生微妙的变化。平时能听到的对岸鸡叫声消失了，取而代之的是近处更细微的声响——露珠从屋檐滴落的声音，邻居家的猫踩在湿漉漉的石板上的脚步声，江水轻轻拍打岸边石头的节奏。雾像一层滤镜，把远处的声音吸收了，把近处的声音放大了。大概七八点钟，太阳升高以后，雾会在十几分钟内迅速散去。这个过程也很美：先是对岸的屋顶从雾中浮现，然后是树梢，最后整条江面像被揭开了面纱一样，突然变得清澈透亮。外婆去世以后我再没回过那个小镇。但每个起雾的清晨，无论我在哪个城市，我都会想起那条江，想起雾中那种温柔的、包裹一切的沉默。
text_pinyin: Xiǎo shíhou zhù zài jiāng biān de wàipó jiā, měi tiān qīngchén wǒ dōu huì bèi yī zhǒng qítè de ānjìng jiào xǐng——bú shì nàozhōng, bú shì jīmíng, ér shì wùqì jiànglín shí shìjiè tūrán biàn de géwài chénjì de nà zhǒng gǎnjué. Tuī kāi chuānghù, jiāng miàn shàng pū zhe yī céng rǔbái sè de báo wù, duì'àn de fángwū hé shùmù zhǐ shèng xia móhu de lúnkuò, xiàng yī fú hái méi huà wán de shuǐmò huà. Wàipó shuō, wù shì jiāng shuǐ zài hūxī. Yè lǐ shuǐwēn bǐ kōngqì gāo, jiāng miàn zhēngténg chū de shuǐqì zài lěng kōngqì zhōng níngjié, jiù xíngchéng le wù. Tā yòng zuì pǔsù de yǔyán, wúyì zhōng gěi le wǒ yī gè xiāngdāng zhǔnquè de qìxiàng xué jiěshì. Wù zhōng de shēngyīn yě huì fāshēng wēimiào de biànhuà. Píngshí néng tīng dào de duì'àn jī jiào shēng xiāoshī le, qǔ ér dài zhī de shì jìn chù gèng xìwēi de shēngxiǎng——lùzhū cóng wūyán dī luò de shēngyīn, línjū jiā de māo cǎi zài shīlùlù de shíbǎn shàng de jiǎobù shēng, jiāng shuǐ qīngqīng pāidǎ àn biān shítou de jiézòu. Wù xiàng yī céng lǜjìng, bǎ yuǎn chù de shēngyīn xīshōu le, bǎ jìn chù de shēngyīn fàngdà le. Dàgài qī bā diǎn zhōng, tàiyáng shēng gāo yǐhòu, wù huì zài shí jǐ fēnzhōng nèi xùnsù sàn qù. Zhège guòchéng yě hěn měi: xiān shì duì'àn de wūdǐng cóng wù zhōng fúxiàn, ránhòu shì shùshāo, zuìhòu zhěng tiáo jiāng miàn xiàng bèi jiē kāi le miànshā yīyàng, tūrán biàn de qīngchè tòu liàng. Wàipó qùshì yǐhòu wǒ zài méi huí guò nàge xiǎo zhèn. Dàn měi gè qǐ wù de qīngchén, wúlùn wǒ zài nǎge chéngshì, wǒ dōu huì xiǎng qǐ nà tiáo jiāng, xiǎng qǐ wù zhōng nà zhǒng wēnróu de, bāoguǒ yīqiè de chénmò.
text_en: When I was little and living at my grandmother's house by the river, every morning I would be woken by a peculiar kind of silence — not an alarm clock, not a rooster's crow, but the feeling of the world growing extraordinarily still when the fog descended. Opening the window, the river surface was draped in a layer of milky-white mist; the houses and trees on the opposite bank were reduced to blurred silhouettes, like an unfinished ink wash painting. Grandmother said the fog was the river breathing. At night the water temperature was higher than the air, and the vapor rising from the river surface condensed in the cold air to form fog. In the plainest language, she had unwittingly given me a remarkably accurate meteorological explanation. Sounds in the fog also changed subtly. The roosters crowing across the bank, normally audible, disappeared, replaced by finer sounds nearby — dewdrops falling from the eaves, the neighbor's cat stepping on damp stone slabs, the rhythm of river water gently lapping against the shore rocks. The fog acted like a filter, absorbing distant sounds and amplifying near ones. Around seven or eight, after the sun climbed higher, the fog would dissipate rapidly within a dozen minutes. This process was beautiful too: first the rooftops on the opposite bank emerged from the mist, then the treetops, and finally the entire river surface seemed to have its veil lifted, suddenly turning clear and luminous. After Grandmother passed away I never returned to that small town. But every foggy morning, no matter which city I am in, I think of that river, and that tender, all-enveloping silence within the fog.

title: A Librarian's Favorite Corner
title_zh: 图书管理员最喜欢的角落
text_zh: 我在市图书馆工作了十七年，有些事情只有长年待在这里的人才会知道。比如，整座图书馆里最安静的地方并不是阅览室——阅览室有翻书声、有空调的嗡鸣、偶尔还有人压低嗓子打电话。真正最安静的地方是三楼东侧尽头的外文期刊区。那里常年光线昏暗，因为窗外有一棵巨大的银杏树，枝叶繁茂到几乎遮住了整面窗户。架子上陈列的是过期的法文和德文学术期刊，几乎没有人来翻阅。但正因如此，那个角落保留了一种珍贵的寂静——不是死寂，而是沉淀了无数个下午的、有厚度的宁静。我习惯在午休时间去那里坐一会儿。靠窗有一把旧藤椅，不知道是哪一任馆长留下的，坐垫已经被压出了一个人形的凹陷。秋天的时候，银杏叶变黄，午后的阳光透过金色的叶片洒进来，整个角落被染成琥珀色。那种光线有一种催眠的力量，让人在不知不觉中放松下来，思绪变得缓慢而透明。有同事问我为什么不申请调到新馆区去，那边设施更新、空间更大。我笑笑说：「新馆很好，但那里没有我的藤椅，没有我的银杏树。」有些东西是搬不走的，因为它们不属于任何人，只属于那个特定的角落和在那里度过的时间。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài shì túshūguǎn gōngzuò le shíqī nián, yǒu xiē shìqíng zhǐyǒu cháng nián dāi zài zhèlǐ de rén cái huì zhīdào. Bǐrú, zhěng zuò túshūguǎn lǐ zuì ānjìng de dìfāng bìng bú shì yuèlǎn shì——yuèlǎn shì yǒu fān shū shēng, yǒu kōngtiáo de wēngmíng, ǒu'ěr hái yǒu rén yā dī sǎngzi dǎ diànhuà. Zhēnzhèng zuì ānjìng de dìfāng shì sān lóu dōng cè jìntóu de wàiwén qīkān qū. Nàlǐ cháng nián guāngxiàn hūn'àn, yīnwèi chuāng wài yǒu yī kē jùdà de yínxìng shù, zhīyè fánmào dào jīhū zhēzhù le zhěng miàn chuānghù. Jiàzi shàng chénliè de shì guòqī de Fǎwén hé Déwén xuéshù qīkān, jīhū méiyǒu rén lái fānyuè. Dàn zhèng yīn rúcǐ, nàge jiǎoluò bǎoliú le yī zhǒng zhēnguì de jìjìng——bú shì sǐjì, ér shì chéndiàn le wúshù gè xiàwǔ de, yǒu hòudù de níngjìng. Wǒ xíguàn zài wǔxiū shíjiān qù nàlǐ zuò yīhuìr. Kào chuāng yǒu yī bǎ jiù téng yǐ, bù zhīdào shì nǎ yī rèn guǎnzhǎng liú xià de, zuòdiàn yǐjīng bèi yā chū le yī gè rénxíng de āoxiàn. Qiūtiān de shíhou, yínxìng yè biàn huáng, wǔhòu de yángguāng tòu guò jīnsè de yèpiàn sǎ jìnlái, zhěnggè jiǎoluò bèi rǎn chéng hǔpò sè. Nà zhǒng guāngxiàn yǒu yī zhǒng cuīmián de lìliang, ràng rén zài bùzhībùjué zhōng fàngsōng xiàlái, sīxù biàn de huǎnmàn ér tòumíng. Yǒu tóngshì wèn wǒ wèishéme bù shēnqǐng diào dào xīn guǎn qū qù, nàbiān shèshī gēng xīn, kōngjiān gèng dà. Wǒ xiàoxiao shuō:「Xīn guǎn hěn hǎo, dàn nàlǐ méiyǒu wǒ de téng yǐ, méiyǒu wǒ de yínxìng shù.」Yǒu xiē dōngxi shì bān bù zǒu de, yīnwèi tāmen bù shǔyú rènhé rén, zhǐ shǔyú nàge tèdìng de jiǎoluò hé zài nàlǐ dùguò de shíjiān.
text_en: I have worked at the city library for seventeen years, and there are things only someone who has stayed here that long would know. For instance, the quietest spot in the entire library is not the reading room — the reading room has the sound of pages turning, the hum of air conditioning, and occasionally someone making a phone call in a lowered voice. The truly quietest place is the foreign periodicals section at the far end of the third floor's east wing. The lighting there is perpetually dim because outside the window stands an enormous ginkgo tree, its branches and leaves so lush they nearly block the entire window. The shelves display expired French and German academic journals that almost nobody comes to read. But precisely because of this, that corner has preserved a kind of precious stillness — not the dead silence of emptiness, but a quiet with depth, one that has settled through countless afternoons. I make a habit of sitting there during my lunch break. By the window there is an old rattan chair, left behind by some former director — the cushion has already been pressed into the shape of a human body. In autumn, when the ginkgo leaves turn yellow, afternoon sunlight filters through the golden leaves and spills inside, dyeing the entire corner amber. That quality of light has a hypnotic power; it lets you relax without realizing it, your thoughts becoming slow and transparent. A colleague once asked why I didn't apply for transfer to the new branch — newer facilities, more space. I smiled and said: 'The new branch is wonderful, but it doesn't have my rattan chair, or my ginkgo tree.' Some things cannot be moved, because they don't belong to anyone — they belong only to that particular corner and the time spent there.

title: The Warmth of a Stranger's Nod
title_zh: 陌生人点头之间的温暖
text_zh: 在我住的小区里，有一个遛狗的老先生，我不知道他姓什么，不知道他住哪栋楼，甚至不知道他养的那条柴犬叫什么名字。但每天早上七点半左右，我出门去地铁站的路上，总会在小区花园的石子路上遇到他。我们之间的全部交流，就是彼此点一下头。不说「早上好」，不说「今天天气不错」，只是微微颔首，然后各走各的路。这种默契持续了大概三年。奇怪的是，这个极其简短的仪式却给了我一种难以言喻的安定感。好像只要早上出门能看到他，今天就会是正常的、平安的一天。有一次我出差两周回来，第一天早上出门又在老地方看到他，他照常点了一下头。那一刻我心里涌起一股温暖——不是那种热烈的情感，而是一种低温的、持续的、像恒温器一样稳定的安心。后来有一阵子我连续好几天没看到他，心里竟然隐隐有些担忧。第四天早上他又出现了，身边换了一条黑色的拉布拉多。他朝我点头的时候，我发现他的眼神比以前柔和了一些，带着某种我读不懂的东西。也许那条柴犬离开了。我们依然没有说话。但那天的点头比平时慢了半拍，好像彼此都在这个微小的动作里多停留了一瞬。我想，人与人之间最珍贵的联结，有时候恰恰不需要语言。
text_pinyin: Zài wǒ zhù de xiǎoqū lǐ, yǒu yī gè liù gǒu de lǎo xiānsheng, wǒ bù zhīdào tā xìng shénme, bù zhīdào tā zhù nǎ dòng lóu, shènzhì bù zhīdào tā yǎng de nà tiáo cháiquǎn jiào shénme míngzì. Dàn měi tiān zǎoshang qī diǎn bàn zuǒyòu, wǒ chū mén qù dìtiě zhàn de lùshang, zǒng huì zài xiǎoqū huāyuán de shízǐ lù shàng yù dào tā. Wǒmen zhījiān de quánbù jiāoliú, jiùshì bǐcǐ diǎn yīxià tóu. Bù shuō「zǎoshang hǎo」, bù shuō「jīntiān tiānqì búcuò」, zhǐshì wēiwēi hànshǒu, ránhòu gè zǒu gè de lù. Zhè zhǒng mòqì chíxù le dàgài sān nián. Qíguài de shì, zhège jíqí jiǎnduǎn de yíshì què gěi le wǒ yī zhǒng nányǐ yányù de āndìng gǎn. Hǎoxiàng zhǐyào zǎoshang chū mén néng kàn dào tā, jīntiān jiù huì shì zhèngcháng de, píng'ān de yī tiān. Yǒu yī cì wǒ chūchāi liǎng zhōu huílái, dì yī tiān zǎoshang chū mén yòu zài lǎo dìfāng kàn dào tā, tā zhàocháng diǎn le yīxià tóu. Nà yī kè wǒ xīnlǐ yǒng qǐ yī gǔ wēnnuǎn——bú shì nà zhǒng rèliè de qínggǎn, ér shì yī zhǒng dīwēn de, chíxù de, xiàng héngwēnqì yīyàng wěndìng de ānxīn. Hòulái yǒu yī zhènzi wǒ liánxù hǎo jǐ tiān méi kàn dào tā, xīnlǐ jìngrán yǐnyǐn yǒu xiē dānyōu. Dì sì tiān zǎoshang tā yòu chūxiàn le, shēnbiān huàn le yī tiáo hēisè de lābùlāduō. Tā cháo wǒ diǎntóu de shíhou, wǒ fāxiàn tā de yǎnshén bǐ yǐqián róuhé le yīxiē, dài zhe mǒu zhǒng wǒ dú bù dǒng de dōngxi. Yěxǔ nà tiáo cháiquǎn líkāi le. Wǒmen yīrán méiyǒu shuōhuà. Dàn nà tiān de diǎntóu bǐ píngshí màn le bàn pāi, hǎoxiàng bǐcǐ dōu zài zhège wēixiǎo de dòngzuò lǐ duō tíngliú le yī shùn. Wǒ xiǎng, rén yǔ rén zhījiān zuì zhēnguì de liánjié, yǒu shíhou qiàqià bú xūyào yǔyán.
text_en: In the residential compound where I live, there is an old gentleman who walks his dog. I don't know his surname, don't know which building he lives in, don't even know the name of the Shiba Inu he keeps. But every morning around seven-thirty, on my way out to the subway station, I always run into him on the gravel path through the compound's garden. The entirety of our communication is a mutual nod. No 'good morning,' no 'nice weather today' — just a slight inclination of the head, then each going our separate way. This unspoken understanding has continued for roughly three years. Strangely, this exceedingly brief ritual gave me a sense of stability difficult to put into words. As long as I could see him when I stepped out in the morning, today would be a normal, safe day. Once I returned from a two-week business trip, and on the first morning back I saw him at the usual spot; he nodded as always. In that moment a warmth welled up inside me — not the kind of ardent emotion, but a low-temperature, sustained, thermostat-like steadiness of reassurance. Later there was a stretch when I didn't see him for several consecutive days, and a faint worry actually crept into my mind. On the fourth morning he reappeared, this time with a black Labrador beside him. When he nodded toward me, I noticed his gaze had softened somewhat, carrying something I couldn't quite read. Perhaps the Shiba had gone. We still didn't speak. But that day's nod was half a beat slower than usual, as if we had both lingered an extra instant within that tiny gesture. I think the most precious connections between people sometimes require no language at all.

title: Tea Leaves Unfurling
text_zh: 父亲泡茶有一套固定的流程，几十年来从未改变。先用沸水烫壶，倒掉，再放入茶叶，注水至七分满，盖上壶盖，等待。他说泡茶最关键的不是水温，不是茶叶的品质，而是等待的耐心。太急着倒出来，茶叶还没有完全舒展，滋味是浅的、涩的；等得太久，又会苦。恰到好处的那个时刻，需要经验，也需要直觉。小时候我不理解他为什么每天花那么多时间在泡茶上。一杯茶而已，速溶的不是也能喝吗？他没有反驳，只是把泡好的茶递给我一杯。我喝了一口，确实跟速溶的不一样——不是简单的好喝或不好喝的区别，而是一种质感上的差异，像手工织布和机器织布的区别。后来我自己开始泡茶，才慢慢理解了父亲的那套仪式。泡茶的过程本身就是目的。当你站在那里等待茶叶在热水中缓缓舒展的时候，你被迫慢下来。你的注意力从纷乱的思绪中抽离出来，集中在眼前这个小小的壶里正在发生的变化上。卷缩的叶片一点点打开，颜色从深褐变成碧绿，水的颜色也渐渐变深。这个过程安静、缓慢、不可逆，像某种微型的四季更迭。父亲去年走了。现在每天早上泡茶的时候，我用的是他留下的那把紫砂壶。壶壁上积了几十年的茶垢，据说这样的壶即使只加白水也能泡出淡淡的茶香。我不知道这是不是真的，但我愿意相信。
text_pinyin: Fùqīn pào chá yǒu yī tào gùdìng de liúchéng, jǐ shí nián lái cóng wèi gǎibiàn. Xiān yòng fèishuǐ tàng hú, dào diào, zài fàng rù cháyè, zhù shuǐ zhì qī fēn mǎn, gài shàng hú gài, děngdài. Tā shuō pào chá zuì guānjiàn de bú shì shuǐwēn, bú shì cháyè de pǐnzhì, ér shì děngdài de nàixīn. Tài jí zhe dào chūlái, cháyè hái méiyǒu wánquán shūzhǎn, zīwèi shì qiǎn de, sè de; děng de tài jiǔ, yòu huì kǔ. Qià dào hǎo chù de nàge shíkè, xūyào jīngyàn, yě xūyào zhíjué. Xiǎo shíhou wǒ bù lǐjiě tā wèishéme měi tiān huā nàme duō shíjiān zài pào chá shàng. Yī bēi chá éryǐ, sùróng de bú shì yě néng hē ma? Tā méiyǒu fǎnbó, zhǐshì bǎ pào hǎo de chá dì gěi wǒ yī bēi. Wǒ hē le yī kǒu, quèshí gēn sùróng de bù yīyàng——bú shì jiǎndān de hǎo hē huò bù hǎo hē de qūbié, ér shì yī zhǒng zhìgǎn shàng de chāyì, xiàng shǒugōng zhī bù hé jīqì zhī bù de qūbié. Hòulái wǒ zìjǐ kāishǐ pào chá, cái mànman lǐjiě le fùqīn de nà tào yíshì. Pào chá de guòchéng běnshēn jiùshì mùdì. Dāng nǐ zhàn zài nàlǐ děngdài cháyè zài rè shuǐ zhōng huǎnhuǎn shūzhǎn de shíhou, nǐ bèi pò màn xiàlái. Nǐ de zhùyì lì cóng fēnluàn de sīxù zhōng chōulí chūlái, jízhōng zài yǎnqián zhège xiǎo xiǎo de hú lǐ zhèngzài fāshēng de biànhuà shàng. Juǎnsuō de yèpiàn yīdiǎndiǎn dǎkāi, yánsè cóng shēn hè biàn chéng bìlǜ, shuǐ de yánsè yě jiànjiàn biàn shēn. Zhège guòchéng ānjìng, huǎnmàn, bùkě nì, xiàng mǒu zhǒng wēixíng de sìjì gēngdié. Fùqīn qùnián zǒu le. Xiànzài měi tiān zǎoshang pào chá de shíhou, wǒ yòng de shì tā liú xià de nà bǎ zǐshā hú. Hú bì shàng jī le jǐ shí nián de chá gòu, jùshuō zhèyàng de hú jíshǐ zhǐ jiā bái shuǐ yě néng pào chū dàndàn de chá xiāng. Wǒ bù zhīdào zhè shì bú shì zhēn de, dàn wǒ yuànyì xiāngxìn.
text_en: Father had a fixed routine for brewing tea that never changed over several decades. First scald the pot with boiling water, pour it out, then add the tea leaves, fill with water to seven-tenths, put on the lid, and wait. He said the most crucial thing about brewing tea isn't the water temperature, isn't the quality of the leaves, but the patience to wait. Pour too hastily and the leaves haven't fully unfurled — the flavor is shallow, astringent; wait too long and it turns bitter. That moment of just right requires experience and intuition alike. As a child I didn't understand why he spent so much time each day on tea. It's just a cup of tea — can't you drink instant? He didn't argue, just handed me a cup of the properly brewed tea. I took a sip and indeed it was different from instant — not a simple distinction of tastes good or doesn't, but a difference in texture, like the difference between hand-woven and machine-woven cloth. Later when I started brewing tea myself, I gradually understood Father's ritual. The process of brewing is itself the purpose. When you stand there waiting for the leaves to slowly unfurl in the hot water, you are forced to slow down. Your attention pulls free from tangled thoughts and focuses on the small changes happening inside that little pot before you. The curled leaves open bit by bit, their color shifting from dark brown to jade green, the water too gradually deepening. This process is quiet, slow, irreversible — like some miniature turning of the seasons. Father passed away last year. Now each morning when I brew tea, I use the purple clay teapot he left behind. The pot walls have accumulated decades of tea stains; they say such a pot can yield a faint tea fragrance even with plain water. I don't know if that's true, but I choose to believe it.

title: A Letter Never Sent
text_zh: 整理母亲遗物的时候，我在她梳妆台的抽屉最深处发现了一封信。信封上写着一个地址，贴了邮票，但从未被投进邮筒。收信人是一个我从没听过的名字：周若兰。信已经被折叠得很整齐，纸张泛黄，墨迹微微褪色，估计写了至少二十年。我犹豫了很久，最终还是打开了。信的内容出乎我的意料。不是什么惊天动地的秘密，只是一封非常平淡的近况报告——母亲告诉周若兰她最近在学做糕点，丈夫升了职，女儿（也就是我）开始上小学了。信的结尾写道：「我时常想起我们在宿舍里彻夜聊天的日子。那时候觉得未来很远，现在回头看，才发现那些夜晚才是最珍贵的。希望你一切都好。」我试着查了那个地址，早已拆迁。我在网上搜索周若兰这个名字，没有任何结果。母亲为什么写了这封信却没有寄出？也许是地址不确定，怕寄不到。也许是写完以后重新读了一遍，觉得措辞不够好，打算改了再寄，结果一拖就忘了。也许她根本就没打算寄——写信本身就是目的，像是对着一个遥远的影子说话，不需要回应，只需要倾诉。我把信重新折好，放回了抽屉。有些话，也许说出来就够了，不一定非要被听到。
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ mǔqīn yíwù de shíhou, wǒ zài tā shūzhuāng tái de chōuti zuì shēn chù fāxiàn le yī fēng xìn. Xìnfēng shàng xiě zhe yī gè dìzhǐ, tiē le yóupiào, dàn cóng wèi bèi tóu jìn yóutǒng. Shōuxìn rén shì yī gè wǒ cóng méi tīng guò de míngzì: Zhōu Ruòlán. Xìn yǐjīng bèi zhédié de hěn zhěngqí, zhǐzhāng fàn huáng, mòjì wēiwēi tuìsè, gūjì xiě le zhìshǎo èrshí nián. Wǒ yóuyù le hěn jiǔ, zuìzhōng háishì dǎkāi le. Xìn de nèiróng chūhū wǒ de yìliào. Bú shì shénme jīngtiān dòngdì de mìmì, zhǐshì yī fēng fēicháng píngdàn de jìnkuàng bàogào——mǔqīn gàosù Zhōu Ruòlán tā zuìjìn zài xué zuò gāodiǎn, zhàngfu shēng le zhí, nǚ'ér (yě jiùshì wǒ) kāishǐ shàng xiǎoxué le. Xìn de jiéwěi xiě dào:「Wǒ shícháng xiǎng qǐ wǒmen zài sùshè lǐ chèyè liáotiān de rìzi. Nà shíhou juéde wèilái hěn yuǎn, xiànzài huítóu kàn, cái fāxiàn nàxiē yèwǎn cái shì zuì zhēnguì de. Xīwàng nǐ yīqiè dōu hǎo.」Wǒ shì zhe chá le nàge dìzhǐ, zǎo yǐ chāiqiān. Wǒ zài wǎng shàng sōusuǒ Zhōu Ruòlán zhège míngzì, méiyǒu rènhé jiéguǒ. Mǔqīn wèishéme xiě le zhè fēng xìn què méiyǒu jì chū? Yěxǔ shì dìzhǐ bú quèdìng, pà jì bú dào. Yěxǔ shì xiě wán yǐhòu chóngxīn dú le yī biàn, juéde cuòcí búgòu hǎo, dǎsuàn gǎi le zài jì, jiéguǒ yī tuō jiù wàng le. Yěxǔ tā gēnběn jiù méi dǎsuàn jì——xiě xìn běnshēn jiùshì mùdì, xiàng shì duì zhe yī gè yáoyuǎn de yǐngzi shuōhuà, bú xūyào huíyìng, zhǐ xūyào qīngsù. Wǒ bǎ xìn chóngxīn zhé hǎo, fàng huí le chōuti. Yǒu xiē huà, yěxǔ shuō chūlái jiù gòu le, bú yīdìng fēi yào bèi tīng dào.
text_en: While sorting through Mother's belongings, I found a letter at the very back of her vanity table drawer. The envelope bore an address and a stamp, but it had never been dropped into a mailbox. The addressee was a name I had never heard: Zhou Ruolan. The letter had been neatly folded, the paper yellowed, the ink slightly faded — written at least twenty years ago, I estimated. I hesitated for a long time but finally opened it. The contents caught me off guard. It wasn't some earth-shattering secret, just a very ordinary update — Mother telling Zhou Ruolan she had been learning to bake pastries lately, that her husband had been promoted, that her daughter (me) had started elementary school. The letter ended: 'I often think of those days we stayed up all night talking in the dormitory. Back then the future seemed so far away; looking back now, I realize those nights were the most precious. I hope all is well with you.' I tried looking up that address — long since demolished. I searched for the name Zhou Ruolan online — no results. Why had Mother written this letter but never sent it? Perhaps the address was uncertain and she feared it wouldn't arrive. Perhaps after finishing she reread it, felt the wording wasn't good enough, planned to revise and send it later, and then let it slip away through procrastination. Perhaps she never intended to send it at all — writing was itself the purpose, like speaking to a distant shadow, needing no reply, only the act of confiding. I refolded the letter and placed it back in the drawer. Some words, perhaps, need only be spoken — they don't necessarily have to be heard.

title: The Rhythm of a Calligrapher's Brush
text_zh: 楼下的赵老先生每天清晨在小区广场上用水写书法。他用的是一支特制的海绵笔，蘸清水，在灰色的水泥地面上写大字。字写完以后，随着水分蒸发，笔画会在几分钟内逐渐消失。他写了几十年，没有留下过一个字。我有一次站在旁边看了整整一个小时。他写字的节奏非常独特——不是匀速的，而是有呼吸感的。起笔的时候很慢，笔尖在地面上停顿了一瞬，好像在酝酿力量。然后行笔加速，流畅地划过，像河水遇到了下坡。到了转折处，又突然减速，甚至完全停住，然后以一个精确的角度拐弯。收笔的时候，笔尖缓缓提起，在空中画出一个看不见的弧线。每一个字都像一次完整的呼吸：吸气、屏息、呼气。我问他为什么不用墨写在纸上，至少可以留下来。他停下笔，看着地上正在消失的字，说：「你看，这个『永』字的最后一笔快要不见了。你觉得它消失了吗？」我说看不见了应该算消失了。他摇摇头：「你刚才看到了。你记住了。它就没有消失。书法的意义不在于那个字最终留在哪里，而在于写的那一刻，你的心、你的手、你的呼吸和那个字之间达成的默契。那个瞬间是永恒的，不需要纸来证明。」
text_pinyin: Lóu xià de Zhào lǎo xiānsheng měi tiān qīngchén zài xiǎoqū guǎngchǎng shàng yòng shuǐ xiě shūfǎ. Tā yòng de shì yī zhī tèzhì de hǎimián bǐ, zhàn qīng shuǐ, zài huīsè de shuǐní dìmiàn shàng xiě dà zì. Zì xiě wán yǐhòu, suízhe shuǐfèn zhēngfā, bǐhuà huì zài jǐ fēnzhōng nèi zhújiàn xiāoshī. Tā xiě le jǐ shí nián, méiyǒu liú xià guò yī gè zì. Wǒ yǒu yī cì zhàn zài pángbiān kàn le zhěngzhěng yī gè xiǎoshí. Tā xiě zì de jiézòu fēicháng dútè——bú shì yúnsù de, ér shì yǒu hūxī gǎn de. Qǐ bǐ de shíhou hěn màn, bǐ jiān zài dìmiàn shàng tíngdùn le yī shùn, hǎoxiàng zài yùnniàng lìliang. Ránhòu xíng bǐ jiāsù, liúchàng de huà guò, xiàng héshuǐ yù dào le xià pō. Dào le zhuǎnzhé chù, yòu tūrán jiǎnsù, shènzhì wánquán tíng zhù, ránhòu yǐ yī gè jīngquè de jiǎodù guǎiwān. Shōu bǐ de shíhou, bǐ jiān huǎnhuǎn tí qǐ, zài kōngzhōng huà chū yī gè kàn bú jiàn de húxiàn. Měi yī gè zì dōu xiàng yī cì wánzhěng de hūxī: xīqì, bǐngxī, hūqì. Wǒ wèn tā wèishéme bú yòng mò xiě zài zhǐ shàng, zhìshǎo kěyǐ liú xiàlái. Tā tíng xià bǐ, kàn zhe dì shàng zhèngzài xiāoshī de zì, shuō:「Nǐ kàn, zhège 'yǒng' zì de zuìhòu yī bǐ kuài yào bú jiàn le. Nǐ juéde tā xiāoshī le ma?」Wǒ shuō kàn bú jiàn le yīnggāi suàn xiāoshī le. Tā yáoyao tóu:「Nǐ gāngcái kàn dào le. Nǐ jì zhù le. Tā jiù méiyǒu xiāoshī. Shūfǎ de yìyì bú zàiyú nàge zì zuìzhōng liú zài nǎlǐ, ér zàiyú xiě de nà yī kè, nǐ de xīn, nǐ de shǒu, nǐ de hūxī hé nàge zì zhījiān dáchéng de mòqì. Nàge shùnjiān shì yǒnghéng de, bú xūyào zhǐ lái zhèngmíng.」
text_en: Old Mr. Zhao downstairs practices calligraphy with water on the compound's plaza every morning. He uses a specially made sponge brush, dips it in clear water, and writes large characters on the gray concrete surface. After the characters are written, the strokes gradually disappear within minutes as the water evaporates. He has written for decades without leaving a single character behind. Once I stood beside him watching for a full hour. The rhythm of his writing was quite distinctive — not uniform, but breathing. The opening stroke was very slow, the brush tip pausing on the ground for an instant as if gathering force. Then the brush accelerated, gliding smoothly across, like a river meeting a downhill slope. At a turning point, it suddenly decelerated, even stopped completely, then turned at a precise angle. At the closing stroke, the brush tip rose slowly, tracing an invisible arc through the air. Every character was like one complete breath: inhale, hold, exhale. I asked him why he didn't use ink on paper, so at least something would remain. He set down his brush, looked at the characters disappearing on the ground, and said: 'Look, the last stroke of this character yong is about to vanish. Do you think it has disappeared?' I said if you can't see it anymore, it should count as gone. He shook his head: 'You just saw it. You remember it. Then it hasn't disappeared. The meaning of calligraphy isn't where the character ultimately rests, but in the moment of writing — the accord between your heart, your hand, your breath, and that character. That instant is eternal. It doesn't need paper to prove it.'

title: Moonlight through Paper Screens
text_zh: 京都的旅馆房间里有一扇障子门——就是那种用木框架糊上和纸的日式拉门。白天的时候，阳光透过纸面洒进来，光线被纸张过滤成一种柔和的、均匀的漫射光，不刺眼，也不阴暗，恰好处于让人舒适的中间地带。但真正让我震动的是夜里。凌晨两点多我醒来，房间一片漆黑，但障子门上映着一片银白色的光。月光。它穿过庭院里的枫树，被枝条切割成碎片，再经过和纸的过滤，变成了一层朦胧的、流动的光影。风吹过的时候，枫树枝轻轻摇晃，纸门上的光影也随之缓缓移动，像一场无声的默片在白色的银幕上放映。我躺在榻榻米上，看了很久。这种光让我想起一个词：「幽玄」——日本美学中对那种隐约的、暗示性的、不完全呈现的美的概括。月光本身已经很美了，但经过纸的过滤和树影的切割之后，它变成了另一种东西：一种被削减了亮度却增加了层次感的美。就像一首诗，最好的意象不是直接说出来的那个，而是被留白和暗示包裹着的那个。第二天退房的时候，前台的老太太问我住得怎么样。我说：「月光很好。」她笑了，好像在说：你看到了。
text_pinyin: Jīngdū de lǚguǎn fángjiān lǐ yǒu yī shàn zhàngzi mén——jiùshì nà zhǒng yòng mù kuàngjià hú shàng hé zhǐ de Rìshì lā mén. Báitiān de shíhou, yángguāng tòu guò zhǐ miàn sǎ jìnlái, guāngxiàn bèi zhǐzhāng guòlǜ chéng yī zhǒng róuhé de, jūnyún de mànshè guāng, bú cìyǎn, yě bù yīn'àn, qiàhǎo chǔyú ràng rén shūshì de zhōngjiān dìdài. Dàn zhēnzhèng ràng wǒ zhèndòng de shì yè lǐ. Língchén liǎng diǎn duō wǒ xǐng lái, fángjiān yī piàn qīhēi, dàn zhàngzi mén shàng yìng zhe yī piàn yínbái sè de guāng. Yuèguāng. Tā chuān guò tíngyuàn lǐ de fēngshù, bèi zhītiáo qiēgē chéng suìpiàn, zài jīngguò hé zhǐ de guòlǜ, biàn chéng le yī céng ménglóng de, liúdòng de guāngyǐng. Fēng chuī guò de shíhou, fēngshù zhī qīngqīng yáohuàng, zhǐ mén shàng de guāngyǐng yě suí zhī huǎnhuǎn yídòng, xiàng yī chǎng wúshēng de mòpiàn zài báisè de yínmù shàng fàngyìng. Wǒ tǎng zài tàtàmǐ shàng, kàn le hěn jiǔ. Zhè zhǒng guāng ràng wǒ xiǎng qǐ yī gè cí:「yōuxuán」——Rìběn měixué zhōng duì nà zhǒng yǐnyuē de, ànshì xìng de, bù wánquán chéngxiàn de měi de gàikuò. Yuèguāng běnshēn yǐjīng hěn měi le, dàn jīngguò zhǐ de guòlǜ hé shùyǐng de qiēgē zhīhòu, tā biàn chéng le lìng yī zhǒng dōngxi: yī zhǒng bèi xuējiǎn le liàngdù què zēngjiā le céngcì gǎn de měi. Jiù xiàng yī shǒu shī, zuì hǎo de yìxiàng bú shì zhíjiē shuō chūlái de nàge, ér shì bèi liúbái hé ànshì bāoguǒ zhe de nàge. Dì èr tiān tuì fáng de shíhou, qiántái de lǎo tàitai wèn wǒ zhù de zěnmeyàng. Wǒ shuō:「Yuèguāng hěn hǎo.」Tā xiào le, hǎoxiàng zài shuō: nǐ kàn dào le.
text_en: The room at the Kyoto inn had a shoji door — the kind of Japanese sliding door with a wooden frame covered in washi paper. During the day, sunlight passed through the paper surface and spilled in, filtered into a soft, even diffused light — not glaring, not dim, situated precisely in the zone of comfort. But what truly moved me was the night. I woke a little past two in the morning; the room was pitch dark, but on the shoji door there appeared a wash of silver-white light. Moonlight. It had passed through the maple trees in the courtyard, been cut into fragments by branches, then filtered through the washi into a layer of hazy, flowing light and shadow. When the wind blew, the maple branches swayed gently, and the patterns of light on the paper door shifted slowly with them, like a silent film projected onto a white screen. I lay on the tatami watching for a long time. This quality of light reminded me of a word: yugen — the Japanese aesthetic concept that captures beauty that is suggestive, allusive, never fully revealed. Moonlight is already beautiful on its own, but after being filtered through paper and carved by tree shadows, it becomes something else: a beauty diminished in brightness yet deepened in layers. Just as in a poem, the finest image is not the one stated directly, but the one wrapped in negative space and implication. When I checked out the next day, the elderly woman at the front desk asked how my stay was. I said: 'The moonlight was wonderful.' She smiled, as if to say: you saw it.

title: The Last Page of a Notebook
text_zh: 我有一个习惯：每用完一本笔记本，在翻到最后一页的时候，总会有一种奇怪的仪式感。不是因为最后一页写了什么特别的内容——通常它和前面的页面一样，记着会议纪要或者零散的想法。这种仪式感来自于「结束」本身。一本笔记本从空白到写满，通常需要三到四个月。这段时间里发生的事，有些被记录了下来，有些没有。被记录的部分构成了一个片面的、不完整的日常档案。你翻回去看，会发现三个月前困扰你的那个问题，现在已经完全忘了；上个月某次会议记的要点，当时觉得至关重要，现在读起来平淡无奇。时间就是这样一个奇妙的编辑器，它不断地重新排列事情的优先级，把你以为重要的东西推到边缘，把你忽略的细节提到前面。我最珍惜的往往不是正式的笔记内容，而是页边的涂鸦——开会走神时画的小方块、等电话时随手写的一串无意义的曲线、某天心情很好时在角落画的一朵花。这些痕迹比文字更诚实，因为它们是无意识留下的，没有经过任何编辑和筛选。写完最后一页以后，我会把笔记本合上，放进书架最高层的一个纸箱里。那里已经有二十多本了，按时间顺序排列。我从来不翻旧的本子，但知道它们在那里，就觉得安心。好像过去的时间虽然回不来了，但至少没有完全消散——它被压缩在那些泛黄的纸页之间，安静地等着。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu yī gè xíguàn: měi yòng wán yī běn bǐjìběn, zài fān dào zuìhòu yī yè de shíhou, zǒng huì yǒu yī zhǒng qíguài de yíshì gǎn. Bú shì yīnwèi zuìhòu yī yè xiě le shénme tèbié de nèiróng——tōngcháng tā hé qiánmiàn de yèmiàn yīyàng, jì zhe huìyì jìyào huòzhě língsǎn de xiǎngfǎ. Zhè zhǒng yíshì gǎn láizì yú「jiéshù」běnshēn. Yī běn bǐjìběn cóng kòngbái dào xiě mǎn, tōngcháng xūyào sān dào sì gè yuè. Zhè duàn shíjiān lǐ fāshēng de shì, yǒu xiē bèi jìlù le xiàlái, yǒu xiē méiyǒu. Bèi jìlù de bùfen gòuchéng le yī gè piànmiàn de, bù wánzhěng de rìcháng dǎng'àn. Nǐ fān huíqù kàn, huì fāxiàn sān gè yuè qián kùnrǎo nǐ de nàge wèntí, xiànzài yǐjīng wánquán wàng le; shàng gè yuè mǒu cì huìyì jì de yàodiǎn, dāngshí juéde zhìguān zhòngyào, xiànzài dú qǐlái píngdàn wúqí. Shíjiān jiùshì zhèyàng yī gè qímiào de biānjí qì, tā búduàn de chóngxīn páiliè shìqíng de yōuxiān jí, bǎ nǐ yǐwéi zhòngyào de dōngxi tuī dào biānyuán, bǎ nǐ hūlüè de xìjié tí dào qiánmiàn. Wǒ zuì zhēnxī de wǎngwǎng bú shì zhèngshì de bǐjì nèiróng, ér shì yè biān de túyā——kāi huì zǒushén shí huà de xiǎo fāngkuài, děng diànhuà shí suíshǒu xiě de yī chuàn wú yìyì de qūxiàn, mǒu tiān xīnqíng hěn hǎo shí zài jiǎoluò huà de yī duǒ huā. Zhèxiē hénjì bǐ wénzì gèng chéngshí, yīnwèi tāmen shì wúyìshí liú xià de, méiyǒu jīngguò rènhé biānjí hé shāixuǎn. Xiě wán zuìhòu yī yè yǐhòu, wǒ huì bǎ bǐjìběn hé shàng, fàng jìn shūjià zuì gāo céng de yī gè zhǐxiāng lǐ. Nàlǐ yǐjīng yǒu èrshí duō běn le, àn shíjiān shùnxù páiliè. Wǒ cónglái bù fān jiù de běnzi, dàn zhīdào tāmen zài nàlǐ, jiù juéde ānxīn. Hǎoxiàng guòqù de shíjiān suīrán huí bù lái le, dàn zhìshǎo méiyǒu wánquán xiāosàn——tā bèi yāsuō zài nàxiē fàn huáng de zhǐ yè zhījiān, ānjìng de děng zhe.
text_en: I have a habit: every time I finish a notebook, turning to the last page always brings a strange sense of ceremony. Not because the last page contains anything special — usually it's the same as the preceding pages, meeting notes or scattered thoughts. The ceremony comes from the act of ending itself. A notebook goes from blank to full in typically three to four months. Of what happened during that time, some things were recorded, some were not. The recorded portion forms a partial, incomplete archive of daily life. Flipping back through, you'll find that the problem troubling you three months ago has now been entirely forgotten; the key points from some meeting last month, which felt critical at the time, now read as utterly unremarkable. Time is a marvelous editor that way — it continually reshuffles priorities, pushing what you thought was important to the margins and elevating the details you overlooked. What I treasure most is often not the formal notes but the margin doodles — little squares drawn while zoning out during a meeting, a meaningless string of curves scrawled while waiting for a phone call, a flower sketched in the corner on a day when I was in a good mood. These traces are more honest than written words because they were left unconsciously, without any editing or filtering. After writing the last page, I close the notebook and place it in a cardboard box on the highest shelf of my bookcase. There are already over twenty in there, arranged in chronological order. I never go back through the old ones, but knowing they are there brings me peace. As if the past, though it can't return, has at least not completely dissipated — it is compressed between those yellowing pages, quietly waiting.

title: Autumn Leaves in a Courtyard
text_zh: 我家老宅的院子里有一棵梧桐树，据说是祖父那一辈种下的。每年秋天，梧桐叶变黄脱落，整个院子会被铺上一层金色的地毯。小时候，父亲每天早上都会拿着扫帚把落叶扫成一堆，装进竹篓里。我问他为什么不等叶子全落完了再扫——反正扫完了第二天又会落一层。他说：「扫落叶不是为了让院子干净。院子从来不会真正干净，因为树还活着，还在落叶。扫落叶是为了跟这棵树打招呼——告诉它，我看到了，我知道又是一天了。」这个回答当时让我觉得莫名其妙。但现在我自己住在这个院子里，每天早上拿起扫帚的时候，终于明白了他的意思。扫落叶确实不是一个关于清洁的动作，而是一个关于时间的仪式。每一片叶子的形状、颜色、干燥程度都不一样。刚落下来的叶子还带着柔软的弧度和残存的绿意，已经落了几天的叶子则变得卷曲、干脆，踩上去会发出清脆的碎裂声。你可以通过观察落叶来感受季节的推进：九月的叶子只是边缘微微泛黄，十月的叶子整片金黄，十一月的叶子则开始发褐，带着一种即将归于泥土的沉稳。扫帚划过石板地面的沙沙声，加上偶尔从树上旋转飘下的新叶，构成了一个安静而完整的秋天的早晨。父亲没有教过我任何关于美学或者哲学的东西，但他教会了我扫落叶。现在想来，这也许是同一件事。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lǎo zhái de yuànzi lǐ yǒu yī kē wútóng shù, jùshuō shì zǔfù nà yī bèi zhǒng xià de. Měi nián qiūtiān, wútóng yè biàn huáng tuōluò, zhěnggè yuànzi huì bèi pū shàng yī céng jīnsè de dìtǎn. Xiǎo shíhou, fùqīn měi tiān zǎoshang dōu huì ná zhe sàozhou bǎ luòyè sǎo chéng yī duī, zhuāng jìn zhúlǒu lǐ. Wǒ wèn tā wèishéme bù děng yèzi quán luò wán le zài sǎo——fǎnzhèng sǎo wán le dì èr tiān yòu huì luò yī céng. Tā shuō:「Sǎo luòyè bú shì wèile ràng yuànzi gānjìng. Yuànzi cónglái bú huì zhēnzhèng gānjìng, yīnwèi shù hái huó zhe, hái zài luòyè. Sǎo luòyè shì wèile gēn zhè kē shù dǎ zhāohu——gàosù tā, wǒ kàn dào le, wǒ zhīdào yòu shì yī tiān le.」Zhège huídá dāngshí ràng wǒ juéde mòmíng qímiào. Dàn xiànzài wǒ zìjǐ zhù zài zhège yuànzi lǐ, měi tiān zǎoshang ná qǐ sàozhou de shíhou, zhōngyú míngbai le tā de yìsi. Sǎo luòyè quèshí bú shì yī gè guānyú qīngjié de dòngzuò, ér shì yī gè guānyú shíjiān de yíshì. Měi yī piàn yèzi de xíngzhuàng, yánsè, gānzào chéngdù dōu bù yīyàng. Gāng luò xiàlái de yèzi hái dài zhe róuruǎn de húdù hé cáncún de lǜyì, yǐjīng luò le jǐ tiān de yèzi zé biàn de juǎnqū, gāncuì, cǎi shàngqù huì fāchū qīngcuì de suìliè shēng. Nǐ kěyǐ tōngguò guānchá luòyè lái gǎnshòu jìjié de tuījìn: jiǔ yuè de yèzi zhǐshì biānyuán wēiwēi fàn huáng, shí yuè de yèzi zhěng piàn jīnhuáng, shíyī yuè de yèzi zé kāishǐ fā hè, dài zhe yī zhǒng jíjiāng guī yú nítǔ de chénwěn. Sàozhou huà guò shíbǎn dìmiàn de shāshā shēng, jiā shàng ǒu'ěr cóng shù shàng xuánzhuǎn piāo xià de xīn yè, gòuchéng le yī gè ānjìng ér wánzhěng de qiūtiān de zǎochén. Fùqīn méiyǒu jiāo guò wǒ rènhé guānyú měixué huòzhě zhéxué de dōngxi, dàn tā jiāo huì le wǒ sǎo luòyè. Xiànzài xiǎng lái, zhè yěxǔ shì tóng yī jiàn shì.
text_en: In the courtyard of our old family home stands a parasol tree, said to have been planted by my grandfather's generation. Every autumn the parasol leaves turn yellow and fall, carpeting the entire courtyard in gold. When I was small, Father would take a broom each morning and sweep the fallen leaves into a pile, gathering them into a bamboo basket. I asked him why he didn't wait until all the leaves had fallen before sweeping — after all, a new layer would fall the very next day. He said: 'Sweeping leaves isn't about keeping the courtyard clean. The courtyard will never truly be clean, because the tree is still alive, still shedding. Sweeping leaves is about greeting this tree — telling it: I see you, I know another day has come.' That answer struck me as baffling at the time. But now I live in this courtyard myself, and each morning when I pick up the broom, I finally understand what he meant. Sweeping leaves is indeed not an act of cleaning but a ritual of time. Every leaf is different in shape, color, and degree of dryness. Freshly fallen leaves still carry a soft curve and lingering green; leaves that have been down for several days have curled and dried crisp, crunching sharply underfoot. You can feel the season's advance by observing the fallen leaves: September's leaves are only faintly yellow at the edges, October's are fully golden, November's begin to brown, carrying a settled composure of something about to return to the earth. The swish of the broom across the stone paving, joined now and then by a new leaf spiraling down from the tree, composes a quiet and complete autumn morning. Father never taught me anything about aesthetics or philosophy, but he taught me to sweep fallen leaves. Looking back now, perhaps those are the same thing.

title: The Weight of a Well-Used Key
title_zh: 一把用旧的钥匙的分量
text_zh: 搬家的时候，我从抽屉里翻出了一把旧钥匙。不是现在住的房子的，也不是上一套的——是我大学毕业后租的第一间房子的钥匙。房东当时多给了我一把备用的，退租的时候我还了一把，另一把不知怎么一直留在了身边，跟着我搬了四次家。这把钥匙比普通钥匙重一些。不是因为材质不同，而是因为用得太久，边缘被磨得光滑圆润，握在手里有一种温润的质感，像一块被河水冲刷了很久的鹅卵石。齿痕也变浅了，如果真的拿去开门，恐怕已经不太灵了。但我一直舍不得扔。那间房子在城南的一栋老楼里，二楼，朝北，冬天很冷，夏天倒还凉快。二十平米，一张床、一张桌子、一个衣柜，塞得满满的。厨房在走廊尽头，和隔壁的邻居共用。水管经常漏水，房东永远在说「下周来修」。但那是我第一次拥有属于自己的空间——不是父母的家，不是学校的宿舍，而是一个我用自己赚的钱租来的、可以随时锁上门把全世界关在外面的小房间。每天晚上下班回来，把钥匙插进锁孔、听到「咔嗒」一声的那个瞬间，就是一天中最安心的时刻。那个声音意味着：外面的一切暂时跟你无关了，这里是你的。现在我住的房子大了很多，用的是指纹锁，开门无声无息。方便是方便了，但总觉得少了点什么。也许少的就是那一声「咔嗒」——那个小小的、确认你到家了的声音。
text_pinyin: Bān jiā de shíhou, wǒ cóng chōuti lǐ fān chū le yī bǎ jiù yàoshi. Bú shì xiànzài zhù de fángzi de, yě bú shì shàng yī tào de——shì wǒ dàxué bìyè hòu zū de dì yī jiān fángzi de yàoshi. Fángdōng dāngshí duō gěi le wǒ yī bǎ bèiyòng de, tuì zū de shíhou wǒ huán le yī bǎ, lìng yī bǎ bù zhī zěnme yīzhí liú zài le shēnbiān, gēn zhe wǒ bān le sì cì jiā. Zhè bǎ yàoshi bǐ pǔtōng yàoshi zhòng yīxiē. Bú shì yīnwèi cáizhì bùtóng, ér shì yīnwèi yòng de tài jiǔ, biānyuán bèi mó de guānghuá yuánrùn, wò zài shǒu lǐ yǒu yī zhǒng wēnrùn de zhìgǎn, xiàng yī kuài bèi héshuǐ chōngshuā le hěn jiǔ de éluǎnshí. Chǐhén yě biàn qiǎn le, rúguǒ zhēn de ná qù kāi mén, kǒngpà yǐjīng bú tài líng le. Dàn wǒ yīzhí shě bù de rēng. Nà jiān fángzi zài chéng nán de yī dòng lǎo lóu lǐ, èr lóu, cháo běi, dōngtiān hěn lěng, xiàtiān dào hái liángkuai. Èrshí píngmǐ, yī zhāng chuáng, yī zhāng zhuōzi, yī gè yīguì, sāi de mǎnmǎn de. Chúfáng zài zǒuláng jìntóu, hé gébì de línjū gòngyòng. Shuǐguǎn jīngcháng lòushuǐ, fángdōng yǒngyuǎn zài shuō「xià zhōu lái xiū」. Dàn nà shì wǒ dì yī cì yōngyǒu shǔyú zìjǐ de kōngjiān——bú shì fùmǔ de jiā, bú shì xuéxiào de sùshè, ér shì yī gè wǒ yòng zìjǐ zhuàn de qián zū lái de, kěyǐ suíshí suǒ shàng mén bǎ quán shìjiè guān zài wàimiàn de xiǎo fángjiān. Měi tiān wǎnshang xià bān huílái, bǎ yàoshi chā jìn suǒ kǒng, tīng dào「kādā」yī shēng de nàge shùnjiān, jiùshì yī tiān zhōng zuì ānxīn de shíkè. Nàge shēngyīn yìwèi zhe: wàimiàn de yīqiè zànshí gēn nǐ wúguān le, zhèlǐ shì nǐ de. Xiànzài wǒ zhù de fángzi dà le hěn duō, yòng de shì zhǐwén suǒ, kāi mén wúshēng wúxī. Fāngbiàn shì fāngbiàn le, dàn zǒng juéde shǎo le diǎn shénme. Yěxǔ shǎo de jiùshì nà yī shēng「kādā」——nàge xiǎo xiǎo de, quèrèn nǐ dào jiā le de shēngyīn.
text_en: While moving, I dug out an old key from a drawer. It wasn't for the place I live now, nor the one before — it was the key to the first apartment I rented after graduating from college. The landlord had given me a spare at the time; when I moved out I returned one, and the other somehow stayed with me, following me through four moves. This key is slightly heavier than an ordinary key. Not because the material is different, but because it was used so long that the edges have been worn smooth and round; it sits in the hand with a warm, polished texture, like a river pebble shaped by long years of current. The teeth have grown shallow too — if I actually tried to open a door with it, it probably wouldn't work anymore. But I've never been able to throw it away. That apartment was in an old building in the south of the city, second floor, north-facing, freezing in winter but pleasantly cool in summer. Twenty square meters: a bed, a desk, a wardrobe, all crammed in. The kitchen was at the end of the corridor, shared with the neighbor next door. The pipes leaked constantly; the landlord was forever saying 'I'll come fix it next week.' But it was the first time I had a space that was mine — not my parents' home, not a school dormitory, but a small room I'd rented with money I earned myself, where I could lock the door at any moment and shut the entire world outside. Every evening coming home from work, the instant I inserted the key into the lock and heard that 'click' was the most reassuring moment of the day. That sound meant: everything out there is temporarily none of your concern — this place is yours. The house I live in now is much larger, with a fingerprint lock that opens silently. Convenient, yes, but it always feels like something is missing. Perhaps what's missing is precisely that 'click' — that small sound confirming you have arrived home.
